chunk_summary: Clinical management of helminth infections in pediatric nutrition integrates deworming, anemia care, and reinfection prevention through WASH practices and targeted therapy, emphasizing empirical treatment and household interventions.
learning_objectives: Understand the clinical signs and symptoms of helminth infections in children, including anemia and poor growth.
Learn appropriate dosing and treatment protocols for soil-transmitted helminths and pinworm infections.
Explore strategies for reinfection prevention, including WASH practices and household interventions.
Integrate helminth management into broader anemia and nutrition care frameworks.
key_facts: Albendazole dosing: 200 mg for children aged 1–2 years, 400 mg for children aged ≥2 years as a single dose.
Pinworm reinfection requires a repeat dose after 2 weeks and simultaneous treatment of household contacts.
Hookworm infections contribute to iron loss, necessitating combined deworming and iron repletion for effective anemia recovery.
Persistent symptoms after treatment should prompt stool testing and evaluation for alternate diagnoses.
Reinfection prevention relies heavily on WASH practices, including safe toilet use and hot washing of clothes and bedding.
reference_quote: Deworming rounds reduce population worm burden, but clinic practice still requires a clinical approach to symptomatic helminth infection and reinfection prevention. (WHO eLENA)
document_key_topics: Clinical management of helminth infections in children
Integration of deworming with anemia and nutrition care
